text: This video presents the case of a surgical procedure performed in a sixteen years old girl presenting with a huge mass in the tail of pancreas. The patient complained of abdominal pain. The CT scan demonstrated a ten centimeter lesion including both solid and cystic areas and calcification. Tumor markers were normal. Preoperative diagnosis was solid.
The surgeon exposes the tumor located behind the greater omentum, with the gastrocolic ligament visibly divided to facilitate access.
The surgeon divides adhesions between the posterior gastric wall and the pancreas, followed by the division of the inferior and posterior attachments of the pancreas using surgical instruments.
The surgeon exposes the splenic vein, revealing its location and orientation within the surgical field.

The surgeon isolates the splenic artery and vein, then transects the pancreas with a tumor-free margin using a 60mm green cartridge and GIS stapler. Veins connecting the pancreas to the splenic vein are isolated and divided.
The surgical specimen is placed into an endo bag and extracted through a Pfannenstiel incision. A Jackson-Pratt drain is then positioned in the surgical site.
